result.markdown:
--- title: | Strengthening Trust in Climate Scientists in the US: A Megastudy Approach description: "Trust in climate scientists is a key predictor of belief in climate change and support for mitigation policies, yet we know surprisingly little about how to strengthen it. Existing evidence is scarce and fragmented across studies with different samples, designs, and outcome measures. To address this gap, I am currently leading a megastudy—a large-scale experiment in which many interventions are tested simultaneously against a common control group, using identical outcome measures. Our megastudy tests 20 interventions designed to increase trust in climate scientists. The interventions were selected from 105 proposals submitted by researchers in response to an open call for collaboration. They include, for example, reading about the history of climate science, learning about peer-review, or discussing reasons for distrust with AI chatbots. The study is fielded in the United States, where public beliefs about climate change are highly polarized and climate research faces growing funding pressures. In this talk, I will present preliminary results of the megastudy. Beyond the substantive question of what strengthens trust in climate scientists, I will walk through the process of running a large-scale collaborative experiment of this kind, from the open call through expert review and selection to data collection and analysis. I will reflect on megastudies as a methodological approach for generating comparable, policy-relevant evidence in behavioral sciences." date: 2026-05-21 author: Jan Pfänder format: revealjs: theme: [dark, custom.scss] smaller: true slide-number: true progress: true chalkboard: true history: false include-after-body: section-label.html template-partials: - title-slide.html # incremental: true # code-link: true image: ../images/mpg_logo.png type: presentation toc: false include-in-header: - text: | --- ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ## Overview ::: {.fragment fragment-index=1} 1. Trust in science matters for mitigating climate change ::: ::: {.fragment fragment-index=2} 2. What is a megastudy and why do one? ::: ::: {.fragment fragment-index=3} 3. Outcomes ::: ::: {.fragment fragment-index=4} 4. Interventions ::: ::: {.fragment fragment-index=5} 5. Results (preliminary, N = 3,695) ::: ::: {.fragment fragment-index=6} 6. Methodological challenges ::: ::: {.fragment fragment-index=7} 7. Should we only be doing megastudies? ::: ::: {.fragment fragment-index=8} 8. Outlook/conclusion ::: ::: {.notes} - **1.** …but we don't know much about how to actually strengthen trust in scientists - **3.** Call for interventions; review and selection process - **4.** Average treatment effects (trust in scientists; secondary outcomes, e.g. donations to scientific association; tertiary outcomes, e.g. concern about climate change); subgroup effects - **5.** Multiple comparison; differential attrition ::: # 1. Trust in science matters for mitigating climate change --- ## Mitigating climate change is urgent [@rogeljCredibilityGapNetzero2023; @calvinIPCC2023Climate2023] . . . People who trust scientists (in general) more, also tend to... . . . ... be more likely to accept the scientific consensus on global warming [@bogertEffectTrustScience2024] . . . ... have more accurate beliefs about climate change [@ejazWhoAvoidsClimate2025] . . . ... support climate policies [@colognaRoleTrustClimate2020; @hornseyMetaanalysesDeterminantsOutcomes2016] --- ## A large study across 55 countries found that trust in climate scientists more specifically was the strongest predictor of belief in climate change and support for climate policies [@todorovaMachineLearningIdentifies2025] ::: {.notes} Add a footnote here among how many variables, what kind of variables, and two examples ::: --- ## But... . . . all of this evidence is *correlational*. --- ## How to strenghten trust in scientists? . . . - using open-science practices [@rosmanOpenSciencePublic2022; @songTrustingShouldersOpen2022] . . . - highlighting successful replications [@hendriksReplicationCrisisTrust2020] . . . - communicating uncertainty [@schneiderEffectsCommunicatingScientific2022] . . . - presenting participants with an infographic about the scientific process [@agleyInterveningTrustScience2021a] . . . - have scientists use two-sided rather than one-sided arguments [@hendriksBalanceCredibilityHow2023] . . . - presenting participanats with short biographies of scientists [@hauteaCommunicatingTrustTrustworthiness2024] . . . Few studies on climate scientists more specifically, but see [@palmDontTellMe2020; @orchinikLearningScientistsConsensus2024; @koetkeEffectSeeingScientists2024] ::: {.notes} - after receiving a message about the need for changes in individual behavior or in public policy to address climate change, people tend to trust climate scientists less [@palmDontTellMe2020]. - participants perceived climate scientists as more skilled---a dimension of trustworthiness---after reading a text about the longstanding history and foundations of climate science [@orchinikLearningScientistsConsensus2024]. - Exercising intellectual humility---acknowledging the limits of one's knowledge---increased trust in a fictive climate scientists [@koetkeEffectSeeingScientists2024]. ::: --- ## But... . . .
There are also null results . . . *e.g., in the US, messages tailored to conservatives did not change their trust in scientists [@gligoricPoliticalIdeologyTrust2025]* . . .
And mixed findings . . . *e.g., effects of communicating scientific uncertainties are highly context-dependent, and some studies find negative effects [@schusterHowCommunicationScientific2026a; @vanderblesEffectsCommunicatingUncertainty2020a]* --- ## In summary... - relatively few studies - even fewer on climate scientists specifically - results are hard to compare due to different research designs # What is a megastudy and why do one? --- ## A megastudy is... . . . "a massive field experiment in which many different treatments are tested synchronously in one large sample using a common, objectively measured outcome" [@milkmanMegastudiesImproveImpact2021] . . .
Often takes the form of an "intervention turnament" ::: {.incremental} - a public call for intervention submissions - most promising submissions get tested - the best intervention wins ::: ::: {.notes} It's like the perfect meta-analysis. A risk with meta-analyses is always to compare apples and oranges. ::: --- ## Existing behavioral megastudies have targeted, e.g., ::: {.incremental} - physical exercise [@milkmanMegastudiesImproveImpact2021] - vaccination [@milkmanMegastudyShowsThat2024; @milkman680000personMegastudyNudges2022] - partisan animosity [@voelkelMegastudyTesting252024] - tax report honesty [@zickfeldEffectivenessExAnte2024] - depression [@kaveladzeCrowdsourcedMegastudy122026] - climate advocacy [@goldwertMegastudyBehavioralInterventions2026] - climate action [@sinclairBehavioralInterventionsMotivate2025; @voelkelRegisteredReportMegastudy2026] - support for carbon pricing [@huberCollectiveEvidenceBehavioral2026] ::: ::: {.notes} The not always explicity claim to be a megastudy, but have some recurrent characteristics. - big sample - many intervention arms - often the result of a crowdsourced collaboration tournament ::: --- ## There are even several guides to megastudies [@duckworthGuideMegastudies2022a; @doellMegastudiesTestEfficacy2023; @voelkelHowDesignConduct2024] --- ```{=html}
A research question that is important to you
↓
Does this question also matter to a lot of other people?
No  →
Collaborative megastudy might be hard…
Yes↓
Do you care more about whether something works than why?
No  →
Don't do a megastudy
Yes↓
Are there many existing studies on your question?
Yes↓
Consider a meta-analysis first
↓
Are the studies high quality and comparable?
Yes → Definitely do a meta-analysis first
No  → Consider a megastudy
No↓
Probably search a bit more
↓
Still no studies?
Yes → Consider a megastudy
``` --- ## Steps in a megastudy^[from personal experience]: . . . 1. Pick an outcome that matters to you . . . 2. Set up an advisory board . . . 3. Launch a call for collaboration . . . 4. Review, select and edit the interventions . . . 5. Build the study and write the preregistration . . . 6. Launch A BIG data collection . . . 7. See what works ::: {.notes} 1. ... or 13 outcomes 2. ... a good excuse to randomly message your favorite researchers 6. ... not scary at all ::: # Outcomes ## Primary outcome: Trust in climate scientists . . . A multidimensional 12-item measure [based on @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025] *(scale from 0 (low trust) to 100 (high trust))* . . . - competence (e.g., "How unqualified or qualified are most climate scientists?") . . . - benevolence (e.g., "How uneager or eager are most climate scientists to improve others’ lives?") . . . - integrity (e.g., "How dishonest or honest are most climate scientists?") . . . - openness (e.g., "How unwilling or willing are most climate scientists to be transparent?") ::: {.notes} To ensure that all interventions would focus on this target, we did not communicate secondary or tertiary outcomes to research teams during the call for intervention submissions (see below). ::: --- ## Secondary outcomes . . . Including, e.g., - role of scientists in policy making (e.g., "Climate scientists should actively advocate for specific policies." - disagree/agree) . . . - donation to the American Meteorological Society (AMS) (lottery, between $0 and $10) . . . - signup to climate scientists Katherine Hayhoe's newsletter (Yes/No self-report + click tracking) ::: {.notes} Secondary and tertiary outcomes capture downstream attitudinal and behavioral consequences of trust, as well as related constructs. ::: --- ## Tertiary outcomes . . . Including, e.g., - belief in climate change (e.g., "Human activities are causing climate change" - not accurate/accurate) . . . - individual behaviors (e.g., "Go on less personal air travel" - not likely/likely) . . . - policy preferences (e.g., "Raising taxes on fossil fuels" - oppose/support) ::: {.notes} The distinction between secondary and tertiary outcomes reflects theoretical proximity to the primary outcome — secondary outcomes such as institutional trust and funding perceptions are more directly related to trust in climate scientists, while tertiary outcomes such as behavioral intentions and policy support are more distal. Practical relevance also plays a role: behavioral measures and funding support are designated secondary due to their direct policy implications. We distinguish between outcome groups primarily to contextualize findings in light of multiple comparison concerns. ::: # Call for collaboration --- ::: {#tbl-submissions .cell tbl-cap='Submissions by career stage'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
position n_submissions
Postdoc 35
Graduate Student/PhD Student 34
Principle investigator/Professor 27
Research Assistant 2
Researcher 2
lecturer 2
Adjunct Research Fellow 1
Associate Professor 1
Guest Researcher (postdoc) 1
``` ::: ::: --- ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Submissions by days until deadline.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-submissions-by-day-1.png){#fig-submissions-by-day width=960} ::: ::: # Selection ::: {.cell} ::: --- ## - Each of the 105 submission was independently reviewed by three randomly assigned reviewers from the advisory board ::: {.incremental} - The reviewers had fixed evaluation criteria, but they could subjectively weight them - Because reviewers rated very differently, we standardized scores within reviewers - **`Final score`:** the average of the weighted standardized scores - We picked the 20 highest scoring interventions ::: --- ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Distribtions of weights assigned by reviewers. Each dot corresponds to one reviewer's weight for the respective rating dimension.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-weights-1.png){#fig-weights width=960} ::: ::: ::: {.notes} Reviewers provided ratings on four dimensions: **1. Theoretical grounding** Is the intervention based on sound and convincing theory? **2. Theoretical insight** Would testing this intervention advance theoretical understanding? (Interventions with clear, single mechanisms may offer stronger insight than those combining many mechanisms.) **3. Odds of success** How plausible is it that the intervention will work in our study? Helpful questions include: - Has it been tested before? - Was the context comparable (U.S. sample, setting, outcomes)? - How large were the effect sizes? **4. Practical relevance** How relevant and scalable is the intervention in the real world? (E.g., can it be implemented easily? Is it feasible at scale?) ::: --- ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Distribtions of reviewer scores (weighted averages of the different rating categories). Each dot corresponds to a review. Lines with dot in the middle (above the boxplots) represent the reviewer specific means and their 95% CIs.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-scores-by-reviewers-1.png){#fig-scores-by-reviewers width=960} ::: ::: # Interventions ::: {.cell} ::: --- ## Collaboration and peer-review
Intervention Summary
Interview Prof. Maraun Climate scientist Prof. Douglas Maraun at the University of Graz in Austria stresses the collaborative and self-correcting process of climate science.
Peer-review What makes climate science trustworthy is the process of independent peer-review.
## Scientific methods and results
Intervention Summary
Measurement & modeling (1) Climate scientists use sophisticated measurement and computational modeling techniques to surveil climate and predict how it changes.
Measurement & modeling (2) Climate scientists are primarily natural scientists (e.g., biologists, physicists). They use use sophisticated tools and quantiative methods to measure and predict climate change.
Model accuracy This is an edited version of a real news article showcasing that even old climate models, despite some flaws, were remarkably correct in predicting global warming.
## Applications and impact
Intervention Summary
Extreme weather predictions Showcases how climate science predicts and helps adapting to different extreme weather events (blizzards, floods, wildfires). Takes into account a participant's state and adresses the extreme weather event most common in that state.
Portrait Prof. Cherry Todd Cherry, a scientist focused on climate issues, is integrated in his local community, and does work that is relevant for this community.
## Others' endorsement
Intervention Summary
Corporate reliance Insurance companies and large corporations rely on climate scientists' projections.
Former skeptics Former climate change skeptics Jennifer Rukavina (television meteorologist) and Bob Inglis (former Repubican congressman) explain how they came to change their mind.
## Values
Intervention Summary
Interview Prof. Sebille Prof. Erik van Sebille, a climate scientist and oceanographer at Utrecht University, Netherlands, mentions harmful consequences of climate change on oceans and humans, and how he cares about preventing these consequences.
Value similarity The quiz 'Which Type of Climate Scientist Are You?' highlights dimensions of climate scientists' trustworthiness. By providing participants with their personalized climate scientists profile, the intervention intends to create perceptions of value similiarity and identification.
## LLM-chatbot
Intervention Summary
LLM chatbot (1) LLM-chatbot
LLM chatbot (2) LLM-chatbot
LLM-chatbot (3) LLM-chatbot
## Other
Intervention Summary
Consensus Correcting potential misperceptions on the level of agreement among climate scientists on cliamte change, and climate change related information.
Funding Correcting potential misperceptions on the amount and sources of climate science funding. Showcases that climate science receives relatively little pubilc and private funding.
High public trust Correct potential misperceptions of how many Americans trusts climate scientists. A majority of Americans trusts climate scientists at least to some extent.
Oil industry misinformation Oil companies have spent decades financing large propaganda campaigns to cast doubt on the existence climate change and the credibility of climate scientists.
Scientist community helpers Climate scientists are members of local communities and their work helps local communities in times of cliamte disasters (e.g., floods and wildfires).
Social justice In the United States, the wealthiest 10% of the population are responsible for roughly 40% of the country’s total greenhouse gas emissions. Climate scientists provide evidence to hold the emitters accountable.
# Results ::: {.cell} ::: --- ::: {.callout-warning} Preliminary — N = 3,695 ::: --- ## Descriptives: Trust in climate scientists (single item) ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Pre-treatment single-item trust in climate scientists (0–100). Left: overall distribution (dashed line = mean). Right: density by party (Democrats vs Republicans).](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-desc-trust-1.png){#fig-res-desc-trust width=100%} ::: ::: --- ## Descriptives: Belief in climate change ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Pre-treatment single-item belief in climate change (0–100). Left: overall distribution (dashed line = mean). Right: density by party (Democrats vs Republicans).](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-desc-belief-1.png){#fig-res-desc-belief width=100%} ::: ::: --- ## Descriptives: Trust dimensions ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Distribution of the four trust sub-dimensions (0–100), control group only (N = 344).](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-desc-dimensions-1.png){#fig-res-desc-dimensions width=100%} ::: ::: # Average Treatment Effects --- ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Points: Treatment effect estimates. Whiskers: 95% CIs (uncorrected). Stars: BH-adjusted across the 20 intervention-vs-control comparisons.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-primary-1.png){#fig-res-primary width=100%} ::: ::: --- ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Interventions ordered by their effect on the primary outcome. Cell = estimate; stars = BH-adjusted.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-secondary-1.png){#fig-res-secondary width=100%} ::: ::: --- ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Interventions ordered by their effect on the primary outcome. Cell = estimate; stars = BH-adjusted.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-tertiary-1.png){#fig-res-tertiary width=100%} ::: ::: --- ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Real-money allocation, 0–10 scale (OLS). Whiskers: 95% CIs (uncorrected). Stars: BH-adjusted across the 20 intervention-vs-control comparisons.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-donation-1.png){#fig-res-donation width=100%} ::: ::: --- ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Logistic regression; marginal effects on the probability scale. Stars: BH-adjusted.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-newsletter-1.png){#fig-res-newsletter width=100%} ::: ::: --- ## Newsletter click-through ::: {#tbl-newsletter-clicks .cell tbl-cap='Click-through on the Talking Climate newsletter link. The link was shown to all completers regardless of signup answer. Time values are seconds spent on the external page among those who clicked.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
signup n n_clicked pct_clicked median_seconds mean_seconds
Yes 423 288 68.1% 24 34.2
No 3272 319 9.7% 6 16.7
``` ::: ::: # Subgroup Treatment Effects --- ## Partisan identity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Moderator: partisan identity. Points are group-specific model predictions — the predicted treatment effect on trust within Democrats and within Republicans. Stars = BH-adjusted within outcome (per preregistration).](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-mod-party-1.png){#fig-res-mod-party width=100%} ::: ::: --- ## Partisan importance ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Moderator: partisan importance. Points are interaction estimates from separate models fit per party — one model for Republicans, one for Democrats. BH adjusted jointly across both (40 comparisons per outcome, per preregistration).](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-mod-partisan-importance-1.png){#fig-res-mod-partisan-importance width=100%} ::: ::: # Methodological challenges --- ## Multiple comparison . . . - Some studies account for it [@milkman680000personMegastudyNudges2022], some don't [e.g., @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024] . . . - You can argue it depends on what you consider a "family of tests" . . . - In any case: - if you do null hypothesis significance testing, testing many effects makes it more likely to obtain statistically significant results by chance alone . . . - so, you need some kind of strategy, or you end up doing data dredging --- ## Multiple comparison Statistical accounting for multiple comparison can allevate this issue. . . . However, even if you do it, there are many ways to do this... - adjust across only interventions - adjust between interventions + outcomes - adjust between moderator levels . . . Many degrees of freedom -> specific preregistering is crucial. ::: {.notes} We haven't simulated how different adjustment measures would affect statistical power. Besides statistical corrections, we tried to account for this issue with the primary, secondary, tertiary outcome hierarchy in addition. ::: --- ## Differential attrition . . . - Happens when, after treatment assignment, attrition differs systematically between experimental conditions . . . - The more experimental conditions, and the more different they are, the more likely . . . - Clearly, megastudies are very vulnerable to differential attriton --- ## Differential attrition Example: . . . - Some people are pissed off by interventions trying to convince them of climate scientists' trustworthiness . . . - These people are likely to have relatively low baseline trust . . . - Some interventions may try harder to convince than others (e.g., by being longer) --- ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Sum of all intervention page-submit timers per participant. Conditions ordered by median time on intervention.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-interv-duration-1.png){#fig-interv-duration width=100%} ::: ::: --- ## Differential attrition If low-trust people drop out after longer treatments more than after shorter ones, then... . . . ... long treatment conditions loose a bunch of low trust people (compared to all other conditions) . . . ... their treatment effects get **overestimated** --- ## Evidence for differential attrition in the data? ::: {#tbl-diff-attrition .cell tbl-cap='Post-treatment dropouts by condition.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
condition n_dropouts
Consensus 1
``` ::: ::: # Should we be doing only megastudies? --- ## `+` . . . - high powered, high quality, directly comparable test of many interventions . . . - collaborative science and inclusion of early career researchers . . . - practical relevance ::: {.notes} Megastudies can be the best version of a meta-analysis ::: --- ## `-` . . . - megastudies provide litte insight into how exactly interventions work . . . - while they are somewhat inclusive, they also create inequalities . . . - require massive budgets, so effectively, only researchers in rich institutions can lead them . . . - not so clear how much being a collaborator will contribute to early researchers' career . . . - hot take: megastudies might not always be as practically relevant as they are sold ::: {.notes} Psychological experiments usually have several conditions that allow to make sepcific claims about how and under which circumstances a manipulation works ::: --- ## Megastudies focus on individuals . . . - in an i-frame vs. s-frame debaste [@chaterIframeSframeHow2023], megastudies are clearly **i-frame** . . . - yet, for trust in scientists specifically, we know that structural factors like representation matter a lot [@druckmanRepresentationScienceTrust2025a] --- ## Effect sizes vs. Demographics ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Treatment effects (left) vs. univariate demographic effects (right) on the same outcome scale. Demographic rows are level-vs-reference contrasts from `outcome ~ demographic + condition` OLS with HC2 SEs (condition included as a fixed effect so demographic effects are on the same residual-variance scale as the treatment effects); `age` is rescaled to per-10-years. Shared x-axis.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-primary-vs-demographics-1.png){#fig-res-primary-vs-demographics width=100%} ::: ::: --- ## Effect sizes vs. Demographics ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Treatment effects (left) vs. univariate demographic effects (right) on donation (0–10). Demographic rows are level-vs-reference contrasts from `outcome ~ demographic + condition` OLS with HC2 SEs (condition included as a fixed effect so demographic effects are on the same residual-variance scale as the treatment effects); `age` is rescaled to per-10-years. Shared x-axis.](05_26_mpg_berlin_files/figure-revealjs/fig-res-donation-vs-demographics-1.png){#fig-res-donation-vs-demographics width=100%} ::: ::: # Outlook and conclusion --- ## - Data collection to be completed within the next ~5 weeks - including a follow-up survey to test persistence after ~1 week . . . - What we expect: - small effects - which might not persist . . . - What we do not expect: - solve deep-seeted structural issues that may foster distrust/lack of trust in science among some groups --- ## So why this study then? . . . - small effects may scale . . . - scientists, policy makers, NGOs, journalists need to communicate about climate science --- we might as well help them use effective strategies . . . - our research group plans to translate interventions into real-life with stakeholders # Thank you --- ##### Research team

Madalina Vlasceanu

Sara Constantino

Viktoria Cologna
##### Advisory board

Mike S. Schäfer

Mario Scharfbillig

Naomi Oreskes

Hugo Mercier

Edward Wile Maibach

James Druckman

Cameron Brick

John C. Besley

Sebastian Berger

Marlene Altenmüller
![[Website](https://janpfander.github.io/trust_climate_scientists/)](../images/qr-code.png){width=200px style="position: absolute; top: 0; right: -0;"} --- ## References ::: {#refs} :::

<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
